accountants-and-auditors: Office-based role in corporate finance, public accounting firms, or government. Works with spreadsheets and accounting software daily. Steady income with tax-season overtime spikes. High demand for precision tools, continuing education, and professional certifications (CPA).
actors: Film, TV, theater, or commercial productions performing scripted roles. Irregular project-based employment; significant audition and rejection cycle. Very variable income; most supplement with side jobs; invests in headshots and acting classes.
actuaries: Insurance companies or consulting firms quantifying financial risk using statistical models. Office-based analytical work; multi-year exam process shapes early career. High income; significant investment in actuarial exam materials and FSA/FCAS credentials.
administrative-services-managers: Corporate offices overseeing facilities, office operations, and support staff. Desk-based with facility walkthroughs and vendor management. Stable upper-mid income; budget responsibility creates spending awareness.
adult-literacy-and-ged-teachers: Community colleges, adult education centers, or nonprofits teaching reading and basic skills. Small classroom environment with diverse, often disadvantaged adult learners. Moderate income; mission-driven; invests in adult learning curriculum resources.
advertising-sales-agents: Media companies or ad agencies selling advertising space to clients. Client-facing relationship sales with quota pressure. Commission-driven income; moderate base; spends on client entertainment and professional wardrobe.
advertising-promotions-and-marketing-man: Marketing agencies or corporate brand teams driving campaigns and growth strategy. Fast-paced creative and analytical environment; heavy meeting and presentation load. High income; significant spending on entertainment, events, and staying current with trends.
aerospace-engineering-and-operations-tec: Aerospace manufacturers or military contractors maintaining and testing aircraft systems. Hands-on technical work in hangars and testing facilities; safety-critical environment. Good income; invests in technical certifications and specialized tools.
aerospace-engineers: Defense contractors, NASA, or commercial aviation companies designing aircraft and spacecraft. Office and lab-based with complex simulation and testing work. High income; invests in engineering software licenses and aerospace industry conferences.
agricultural-and-food-science-technician: Agricultural research stations or food companies conducting field and lab tests. Mix of outdoor fieldwork and lab analysis; seasonal rhythms. Moderate income; invests in lab equipment and agricultural science publications.
agricultural-and-food-scientists: USDA, universities, or food companies researching crop production and food safety. Lab and field research environment; grant cycles influence work pace. Good income; invests in scientific journals and field research travel.
agricultural-engineers: Agricultural equipment companies or research institutions solving farming technology challenges. Mix of lab design work and field testing on farms. Good income; values rural lifestyle; spending on agricultural technology and field vehicles.
agricultural-workers: Farms and agricultural operations planting, cultivating, and harvesting crops. Seasonal physically demanding outdoor work; migrant worker pattern common. Low income; often seasonal with minimal savings; sends remittances to home countries.
air-traffic-controllers: Airport FAA towers or enroute centers managing aircraft separation and flow. High-stress, high-concentration shift work; early retirement eligible. High income; strong federal benefits; stressful lifestyle may lead to compensatory spending.
aircraft-and-avionics-equipment-mechanic: Airlines or military bases maintaining aircraft systems and avionics. Safety-critical precision work; shift-based with night shifts common. High income; invests in FAA A&P certification and specialized tools.
airline-and-commercial-pilots: Commercial airlines or cargo carriers operating passenger or freight aircraft. Schedule-driven shift work with hotel layovers away from home. High income; spends on hotel meals, per diems, and aviation gear; values lifestyle flexibility.
animal-care-and-service-workers: Animal shelters, kennels, or zoos caring for animals and interacting with their owners. Physically active animal handling work; emotionally demanding end-of-life situations. Low income; passion-driven career; spends on pet care products.
announcers: Radio stations, sports venues, or event companies providing live commentary and hosting. Performance-based irregular work; often part-time or freelance. Variable income; often supplementary; invests in voice training and audio equipment.
anthropologists-and-archeologists: Universities or government agencies studying human cultures and ancient sites. Mix of fieldwork, often in remote locations, and academic writing. Moderate income; often adjunct or grant-funded; invests in fieldwork equipment.
arbitrators-mediators-and-conciliators: Courts or private ADR firms facilitating dispute resolution outside of litigation. Scheduled session-based work; requires sharp analytical and interpersonal skills. Moderate-to-high income; often second career after law; invests in mediation training.
architects: Architecture firms or corporate real estate departments designing buildings and spaces. Studio-based creative work with client presentations and site visits. Mid-to-high income; invests in design software (AutoCAD, Revit) and professional licensure.
architectural-and-engineering-managers: Engineering or architecture firms leading technical teams on large projects. Mix of office and project site presence; proposal and deadline pressure. High income; spends on professional licenses, technical software, and industry conferences.
curators-museum-technicians-and-conserva: Museums, historical societies, or cultural institutions preserving and displaying collections. Mix of public-facing gallery work and behind-the-scenes preservation. Moderate income; highly educated; invests in academic publications and field conferences.
art-directors: Advertising agencies, publishers, or in-house brand teams leading visual design direction. Creative leadership role with client presentations and team management. High income; significant spending on design inspiration, events, and tools.
assemblers-and-fabricators: Manufacturing plants assembling products on production lines. Repetitive physical work; shift-based including nights; high efficiency pressure. Below-average income; limited job mobility; spends carefully on essentials.
athletes-and-sports-competitors: Professional sports teams or independent competition in competitive athletic events. Training-dominant daily routine; income varies enormously by sport and level. Variable income from elite to minor; significant spending on coaching and equipment.
athletic-trainers: Sports teams, schools, or clinical settings preventing and treating athlete injuries. Active physical environment; early starts for practices and late finishes for games. Moderate income; spends on athletic supplies and NATA certification maintenance.
atmospheric-scientists-including-meteoro: Weather services, research institutes, or TV broadcasting analyzing atmospheric conditions. Shift-based monitoring work with modeling software. Good income; invests in meteorological data subscriptions and weather instruments.
audiologists: Hospitals, clinics, or private practice diagnosing and treating hearing disorders. Quiet clinical environment with specialized diagnostic equipment. High income; invests in audiology equipment and continuing education.
automotive-body-and-glass-repairers: Auto body shops restoring collision-damaged vehicles. Physical shop work with paint and bodywork; insurance-driven workflow. Moderate income; often flat-rate pay tied to efficiency; invests in specialty tools.
automotive-service-technicians-and-mecha: Dealerships or independent auto shops diagnosing and repairing vehicles. Physically demanding shop work; flat-rate pay creates income variability. Moderate income; significant investment in personal toolbox and ASE certifications.
bakers: Commercial bakeries or retail bakeshops producing bread and pastry products. Early morning starts; physically active standing work; hot environment. Below-average income; passion-driven career; spends on specialty ingredients and baking equipment.
barbers-hairstylists-and-cosmetologists: Salons or barbershops cutting and styling hair for walk-in and regular clients. Standing-intensive client-facing work; tips supplement base income. Moderate income with tips; often self-employed or booth renter; invests in quality tools and products.
bartenders: Bars, restaurants, or event venues mixing and serving drinks for customers. Evening and weekend shifts; income heavily tip-dependent. Moderate income with tips; spends on alcohol knowledge, bar tools, and industry networking.
bill-and-account-collectors: Collection agencies or corporate accounts receivable departments recovering unpaid debts. Phone-intensive desk work; high-stress rejection-heavy environment. Moderate income; often commission-based on collections; limited spending flexibility.
biochemists-and-biophysicists: Pharmaceutical companies or research universities studying chemical processes in living systems. Lab-intensive work with specialized equipment; publication pressure in academia. High income in industry; moderate in academia; invests in scientific reagents and journals.
biomedical-engineers: Medical device companies, hospitals, or research institutions developing healthcare technology. Lab and office split; FDA regulatory environment adds process overhead. High income; invests in biotech conferences and engineering journals.
biological-technicians: Research labs or biotech companies assisting scientists with experiments. Hands-on lab work; equipment maintenance and data collection. Moderate income; often entry-level research pathway; invests in lab certifications.
boilermakers: Industrial plants, refineries, or shipyards assembling and maintaining large pressure vessels. Heavy physically demanding work in confined spaces and at heights. Good income with union wages; shift-based and overtime common; spends on tools and workwear.
bookkeeping-accounting-and-auditing-cler: Small businesses or corporate accounting departments maintaining financial records. Desk-based data entry and reconciliation work; seasonal tax workload spikes. Moderate income; invests in QuickBooks certification and accounting software.
broadcast-and-sound-engineering-technici: TV stations, studios, or live events managing audio-visual equipment and broadcasts. Shift-based technical work; live event deadline pressure. Moderate income; invests in audio technology and broadcast engineering certifications.
budget-analysts: Works in government agencies, universities, or large corporations managing spending plans. Desk-based, heavy spreadsheet and data analysis work. Stable salary with predictable hours. Professional wardrobe and commuting costs are regular expenses.
bus-drivers: Public transit agencies or private operators transporting passengers on fixed routes. Shift-based driving with passenger management responsibilities. Moderate income with union wages; invests in CDL endorsements; values job stability.
butchers-and-meat-cutters: Grocery stores or meat processing facilities cutting and preparing meat products. Cold, physically demanding work with sharp cutting tools. Moderate income; union protection in some settings; invests in quality knives and protective gear.
calibration-technologists-and-technician: Manufacturing or lab facilities ensuring measuring instruments meet specifications. Precise bench work with calibration equipment; quality-critical environment. Good income; invests in metrology certifications and precision tools.
cardiovascular-technologists-and-technic: Hospitals or cardiology clinics performing ECG and imaging procedures. Clinical environment with patient-facing hands-on work. Good income; spends on scrubs and clinical certifications.
career-and-technical-education-teachers: High schools or community colleges teaching trade or vocational subjects. Hands-on classroom environment with shop equipment and industry tools. Good income; values practical skills transfer; invests in trade certifications and tools.
carpenters: Construction sites or millwork shops framing structures and installing finish materials. Physically demanding outdoor and indoor work; project-based employment. Moderate-to-good income; invests in hand tools and power tools; values trade independence.
cartographers-and-photogrammetrists: Government agencies or private mapping firms creating geographic data and maps. Office and field data collection work; GIS software is the primary tool. Moderate income; spends on GIS certifications and mapping technology.
cashiers: Retail stores, grocery stores, or fast food scanning purchases and handling transactions. Standing shift work; customer-facing entry-level role. Low income; limited benefits; often supplementary or student income; minimal discretionary spending.
chefs-and-head-cooks: Restaurants or catering operations directing kitchen operations and menu development. Long nights, weekends, and holiday shifts; physically demanding kitchen leadership. Moderate income; invests in culinary courses, knife sets, and industry connections.
chemical-engineers: Chemical plants, pharmaceutical companies, or refineries designing and optimizing processes. Safety-critical industrial environment; mix of plant floor and office design work. High income; values job stability; invests in chemical engineering certifications (PE license).
chemical-technicians: Chemical plants or research labs supporting chemists with testing and analysis. Lab-based technical work with hazardous materials protocols. Moderate income; values hands-on technical work; invests in chemistry certifications.
chemists-and-materials-scientists: Industrial companies, government labs, or universities developing new materials and compounds. Lab-intensive research environment with safety protocols. Good income; invests in ACS membership, lab equipment, and technical journals.
childcare-workers: Daycare centers, private homes, or preschools caring for young children throughout the day. Active physical work; highly nurturing environment; responsible for safety and development. Low income; often part-time or irregular hours; passion for children drives career choice.
chiropractors: Private practice or group clinics providing spinal manipulation and musculoskeletal care. Physically active patient-facing role; builds own client base. Good income but high overhead for private practice; significant student loan burden.
civil-engineering-technicians: Engineering firms or government agencies assisting with infrastructure project design and inspection. Mix of office CAD work and field site inspection. Moderate income; spends on survey equipment and technical continuing education.
civil-engineers: Engineering firms, government, or construction companies designing bridges, roads, and utilities. Office design work with regular project site visits. High income; significant investment in PE licensure, engineering software, and professional organizations.
claims-adjusters-appraisers-examiners-an: Insurance industry role involving fieldwork and office investigation of claims. Frequent travel to accident or damage sites; owns or is reimbursed for vehicle use. Income is stable; stress level can spike with major weather events or high caseloads.
clinical-laboratory-technologists-and-te: Hospital labs or diagnostic centers analyzing blood, tissue, and other specimens. Shift work common; precise technical work behind the scenes. Good income; invests in ASCP certification and lab science continuing education.
coaches-and-scouts: Sports teams at school, college, or professional levels teaching skills and identifying talent. Active, travel-heavy role; game and practice schedule driven. Moderate income; invests in coaching certifications and scouting technology.
community-health-workers: Nonprofit or public health agencies connecting underserved populations to health resources. Community fieldwork; home visits and outreach in challenging environments. Below-average income; mission-driven; limited discretionary spending.
compensation-and-benefits-managers: Large corporate HR functions designing total rewards programs. Desk-based strategic role with heavy vendor and benchmark data work. High income; invests in HR technology and compensation benchmarking subscriptions.
compensation-benefits-and-job-analysis-s: HR-adjacent role in corporate offices managing pay structures and benefits packages. Desk-based with regular meetings and HR software usage. Stable salary; often purchases professional development courses and HR certification prep.
compliance-officers: Works in banks, healthcare, or large corporations ensuring regulatory adherence. Office-based with heavy documentation and legal review. High job security; stress tied to audits. Invests in compliance training courses and industry association memberships.
computer-and-information-research-scient: Research labs, universities, or major tech companies advancing computing science. Office and lab research environment; publication and grant cycles drive work rhythm. High income; early technology adopter; invests in conferences and research tools.
computer-and-information-systems-manager: IT leadership in corporations, government, or tech firms directing tech strategy and teams. Office-based with occasional hands-on infrastructure work. High income; significant spending on tech devices, certifications, and vendor relationships.
computer-hardware-engineers: Tech companies or electronics manufacturers designing chips and hardware systems. Lab and office environment with prototype and testing work. High income; early adopter of consumer electronics; invests in technical skills and conferences.
computer-network-architects: Corporations or tech firms designing network infrastructure for reliability and security. Office-based with occasional data center visits; on-call for outages. High income; heavy investment in networking certifications (CCIE, CCNP) and hardware.
computer-programmers: Software companies, corporate IT departments, or freelance; writing and testing code. Desk-intensive work with long focus blocks; remote work common. Good income; spends on developer tools, online courses, and high-end computing equipment.
computer-support-specialists: IT help desks or field support in corporations, schools, or government agencies. Mix of phone/remote support and on-site hardware work. Moderate income; invests in CompTIA certifications; values career advancement to sysadmin roles.
computer-systems-analysts: Corporations or consulting firms bridging IT and business needs. Office-based with stakeholder interviews and technical documentation. High income; invests in ERP certifications and business analysis courses.
concierges: Hotels or luxury residential buildings assisting guests with reservations and requests. Customer-facing service role; vast local knowledge required. Moderate income; values service; receives tips; spends on personal upkeep for client-facing role.
conservation-scientists: Government agencies or nonprofits managing natural resources and land health. Outdoor fieldwork mixed with report writing and stakeholder communication. Moderate income; environmentally motivated; spends on field gear and certification.
construction-and-building-inspectors: Government agencies or private firms ensuring construction meets building codes. Regular site visits in various weather and construction conditions. Good income; invests in ICC certifications and inspection tools; values authority and compliance.
construction-equipment-operators: Construction sites operating heavy machinery like bulldozers and cranes. Outdoor physical work; seasonal employment in cold climates. Good income with union wages; spends on safety gear and equipment training.
construction-laborers-and-helpers: Construction sites performing manual tasks for skilled tradespeople. Heavy physical outdoor work in all weather conditions. Below-average income; often day labor or seasonal; limited savings capacity.
construction-managers: Construction firms managing projects from blueprints to completion. Split between job sites and office; physical outdoor exposure. High income tied to project pipeline; invests in safety equipment and project management tools.
cooks: Restaurant kitchens or institutional foodservice preparing meals according to recipes. Physically demanding hot kitchen work; evening and weekend shifts. Low-to-moderate income; limited savings; values craft pride; spends on culinary skills.
correctional-officers: Jails or prisons supervising inmates and maintaining facility security. Physically and mentally demanding shift work; safety risks. Moderate income with government benefits; union protection; pension-focused retirement planning.
cost-estimators: Construction, manufacturing, or engineering firms; estimates project costs before work begins. Mix of site visits and office desk work. Project-driven schedule with bidding deadlines. Uses specialized estimation software; income tied to industry cycles.
court-reporters: Courtrooms or deposition settings creating verbatim transcripts of legal proceedings. Intense concentration work; freelance scope depositions often supplement court salary. Moderate income; invests in steno machine and RPR certification.
craft-and-fine-artists: Self-employed studios, galleries, or commissions producing original artwork. Irregular freelance income; studio expenses for materials and rent. Variable and often low income; strong intrinsic motivation; spends on materials and exhibition costs.
credit-counselors: Nonprofit agencies or financial institutions helping clients manage debt and budgeting. Client-facing desk role with phone and in-person consultations. Below-average pay for the sector; mission-driven workers who prioritize helping others.
customer-service-representatives: Call centers, retail, or corporate offices handling customer inquiries and complaints. Phone and computer-based desk work; emotionally taxing interactions. Below-average income; often entry-level; limited discretionary spending.
dancers-and-choreographers: Dance companies, entertainment venues, or schools performing and choreographing dance. Physically demanding practice and performance schedule; short career windows. Variable income; passion-driven; invests in dance training, shoes, and costumes.
data-scientists: Tech companies, financial firms, or corporate analytics teams building predictive models. Desk-intensive coding and analysis work; remote work common. High income; invests in computing hardware, data tools, and online courses (Coursera, Kaggle).
database-administrators: IT departments or cloud service companies managing databases for performance and security. Desk-based with on-call responsibility for outages. High income; invests in database certifications (Oracle, SQL Server) and cloud skills.
delivery-truck-drivers-and-driver-sales-: Package delivery companies or wholesale distributors delivering goods to homes and businesses. Physical loading and driving work; time-pressured route completion. Moderate income; wear on personal vehicle; invests in CDL and route efficiency.
dental-and-ophthalmic-laboratory-technic: Dental or ophthalmic labs fabricating custom prosthetics and lenses. Precision bench work with specialty materials and tools. Moderate income; invests in technical skills and lab equipment.
dental-assistants: Dental offices assisting dentists with procedures and patient care. Clinical chair-side work; close patient contact; variable part-time/full-time schedules. Below-average income; limited savings capacity; values office culture and flexible hours.
dental-hygienists: Dental offices providing teeth cleanings and educating patients on oral hygiene. Part-time schedules common; physically demanding on wrists and back. Good income especially part-time rate; values work-life balance; spends on ergonomic equipment.
dentists: Own practice or group dental offices providing comprehensive oral care. High-focus manual dexterity work with scheduled patient appointments. Very high income; high practice overhead and student debt; spends on high-end equipment and dental CE.
desktop-publishers: Publishing companies or corporate communications departments laying out printed materials. Desk-based creative production work with deadline pressure. Moderate income; invests in Adobe InDesign skills and design publications.
diagnostic-medical-sonographers: Hospitals or imaging centers performing ultrasound scans. Physically demanding scanning work; repetitive motion risk. Good income; values clinical skills; invests in ARDMS certification.
diesel-service-technicians-and-mechanics: Trucking companies, bus fleets, or dealers maintaining heavy diesel engines. Heavy physical shop work; specialized knowledge of diesel systems. Good income; invests in diesel diagnostic tools and ASE Heavy Truck certifications.
dietitians-and-nutritionists: Hospitals, schools, or private practice advising patients on nutrition and diet plans. Mix of patient counseling and meal planning analysis. Moderate income; health-conscious spending habits; invests in RD continuing education.
drafters: Engineering or architecture firms creating technical drawings and plans. Desk-based with heavy CAD software usage. Moderate income; invests in AutoCAD and other design software skills.
drywall-and-ceiling-tile-installers-and-: New construction or renovation projects installing and finishing interior walls. Physical standing and overhead work; exposure to dust and compounds. Moderate income; often self-employed; invests in tools and material vehicles.
economists: Government agencies, think tanks, or universities studying economic trends and policy. Office-based research and writing; data analysis with econometric software. High income in private sector; moderate in academia; invests in data subscriptions.
editors: Publishing houses, newspapers, or corporate communications departments editing written content. Desk-based editorial work; remote work increasingly common. Moderate income; invests in AP Stylebook and editing software tools.
electrical-and-electronics-engineering-t: Manufacturing or engineering firms assisting electrical engineers with design and testing. Hands-on lab and production floor work. Moderate income; spends on electronic tools and technical certifications.
electrical-and-electronics-engineers: Tech, manufacturing, or utility companies designing electrical systems and circuits. Office and lab environment; high precision required. High income; invests in EE software (MATLAB, SPICE) and IEEE memberships.
electrical-and-electronics-installers-an: Industrial plants or utility companies installing and maintaining power systems. Field-based electrical work with travel between sites. Good income; often union wages; invests in electrical testing equipment.
line-installers-and-repairers: Utility companies maintaining power lines and telecommunications infrastructure. Outdoor work at heights on poles and towers; storm response overtime common. High income; union wages with overtime premium; invests in lineman tools and safety gear.
electricians: Residential, commercial, or industrial sites installing and maintaining electrical systems. Mix of indoor and outdoor work; shift and overtime common. Good income with union wages; invests in tools, licensing fees, and code books.
electro-mechanical-technicians: Manufacturing plants or testing facilities maintaining automated and mechanical systems. Hands-on technical work with both electrical and mechanical components. Moderate income; values practical skills; spends on tool kits and technical training.
elementary-middle-and-high-school-princi: K-12 public or private schools managing staff, budget, and student outcomes. Long days balancing parent meetings, staff supervision, and administrative paperwork. Moderate-to-good income; high stress; invests in leadership development and community presence.
elevator-installers-and-repairers: Commercial and residential buildings installing and servicing elevator systems. Physically demanding work in shafts and machine rooms; on-call for emergencies. High income with union wages; values skilled trade status.
emergency-management-directors: Government agencies or large organizations planning and coordinating disaster response. Office-based with high-stakes incident response work. Stable government income; invests in emergency preparedness certifications and drills.
emts-and-paramedics: Emergency medical services responding to 911 calls and providing pre-hospital care. High-stress, shift-based work with physical demands and critical decision-making. Below-average income for the stress level; values camaraderie; limited discretionary spending.
entertainment-and-recreation-managers: Theaters, gyms, parks, or casinos managing venues and programming. Evening and weekend hours common; customer-facing management role. Moderate income; spends on industry events and staff development.
environmental-engineering-technicians: Environmental consulting firms or government agencies conducting field and lab tests. Field sampling and data collection work in varied outdoor settings. Moderate income; mission-aligned work; invests in environmental certification courses.
environmental-engineers: Consulting firms, government agencies, or industrial companies solving pollution and waste problems. Mix of field investigation and office report writing. High income; invests in PE licensure and environmental science conferences.
environmental-science-and-protection-tec: Environmental agencies or consulting firms collecting and testing environmental samples. Outdoor field sampling and lab analysis work. Moderate income; invests in field sampling equipment and environmental certifications.
environmental-scientists-and-specialists: Consulting firms, government agencies, or industrial companies solving environmental problems. Mix of field investigation and office reporting; regulatory compliance focus. Good income; invests in PE licensure and environmental science conferences.
epidemiologists: Public health departments or universities investigating disease patterns and outbreaks. Data analysis and community health assessment; urgent response to outbreaks. Good income; invests in public health certifications and epidemiology software.
exercise-physiologists: Hospitals, rehab clinics, or corporate wellness centers designing exercise programs. Active clinical environment; mix of patient assessment and program delivery. Moderate income; health-focused lifestyle; spends on fitness equipment and ACSM certification.
farmers-ranchers-and-other-agricultural-: Family farms or commercial agricultural operations managing land, livestock, and crops. Early mornings, physical outdoor work, and seasonal income volatility. Capital-intensive business; major spending on equipment, seeds, and fuel.
fashion-designers: Fashion houses, apparel companies, or freelance creating clothing and accessory designs. Creative studio work with fabric sourcing and garment construction. Moderate-to-high income; heavily invested in fashion industry networking and trends.
film-and-video-editors-and-camera-operat: Film studios, production companies, or freelance creating and editing video content. Studio and on-location work; deadline-driven project environment. Moderate-to-good income; significant investment in camera gear and editing software (Premiere, DaVinci).
financial-analysts: Works in investment banks, asset management, or corporate finance departments. Long desk hours with heavy use of financial modeling tools (Excel, Bloomberg). Income includes bonuses; high status and spending on suits, networking, and grad school prep.
financial-clerks: Banks, insurance companies, or corporate offices processing financial transactions. Desk-based data processing with accuracy requirements. Moderate income; values job stability; invests in accounting certifications.
financial-examiners: Government regulators or bank examiners auditing financial institutions. Travel-heavy role visiting financial institutions on-site. Stable government-level pay with strong job security and benefits.
financial-managers: Corporate finance or treasury departments overseeing financial health of organizations. Senior leadership role with board-level reporting and high responsibility. High income with bonus potential; strong spending on business attire and executive lifestyle.
fire-inspectors-and-investigators: Fire departments or government agencies inspecting buildings and investigating fire causes. Field-based inspection work with report writing. Good income; invests in ICC fire certifications and investigation training.
firefighters: Fire stations responding to fires, medical emergencies, and hazmat incidents. Shift-based 24-hour rotations; physically demanding and dangerous work. Good income with benefits; union wages; values team camaraderie; spends on fitness equipment.
fishers-and-related-fishing-workers: Commercial fishing vessels harvesting fish and seafood in lakes, rivers, or oceans. Physically demanding and dangerous work; long trips away from home. Variable income; high-earning in peak season; spends on fishing gear and boat maintenance.
fitness-trainers-and-instructors: Gyms, fitness studios, or independent coaching; leading exercise sessions and personal training. Physically active; early morning and evening peak hours; often freelance. Moderate income; invests in NSCA/NASM certification, fitness equipment, and personal training software.
flight-attendants: Commercial airlines ensuring passenger safety and comfort during flights. Irregular schedule with hotel layovers; physically demanding standing work. Moderate income with travel benefits; takes advantage of flight discounts for leisure travel.
tile-and-marble-setters: Residential and commercial buildings installing tile and stone flooring and surfaces. Physically demanding kneeling and precision work. Moderate income; often self-employed; invests in tile setting tools and materials.
floral-designers: Florist shops or event companies creating floral arrangements. Physically active retail or event production work; irregular hours around events. Low income; often small business; invests in flowers, tools, and AIFD certification.
food-and-beverage-serving-and-related-wo: Quick service restaurants, cafeterias, or food stands taking orders and serving food. Fast-paced customer-facing shift work; variable hours. Low income; often part-time or student employment; limited discretionary spending.
food-preparation-workers: Restaurant kitchens or food production facilities doing prep work for cooks. Repetitive physical work; early morning and split-shift patterns. Low income; often entry-level; limited savings capacity.
food-and-tobacco-processing-workers: Food manufacturing plants operating processing equipment on production lines. Repetitive shift work; regulated food safety environment. Below-average income; shift differential common; limited discretionary spending.
food-service-managers: Restaurants, cafeterias, or catering companies managing operations and staff. Long hours including nights and weekends; high turnover environment. Moderate income; spends on culinary tools, uniforms, and vendor relationships.
forensic-science-technicians: Crime labs or law enforcement agencies analyzing physical evidence. Precise lab work with legal chain-of-custody requirements. Moderate income; invests in ASCLD certification and forensic science training.
forest-and-conservation-workers: Forest service agencies or logging companies maintaining and harvesting timber resources. Outdoor physical work in remote forested areas. Moderate income; values nature connection; spends on outdoor gear and forestry tools.
fundraisers: Nonprofits, universities, or political campaigns; builds donor relationships and solicits gifts. Mix of events, outreach calls, and database management. Moderate pay with income tied to fundraising cycles; high social networking activity.
funeral-service-occupations: Funeral homes managing end-of-life ceremonies and body preparation. Emotionally demanding client-facing service; irregular hours for calls. Moderate income; conservative spending; invests in NFDA membership and embalming certification.
gaming-services-occupations: Casinos or gaming facilities dealing cards, operating equipment, and assisting players. Night and weekend shifts; customer-facing high-energy environment. Moderate income with tips; shift-based lifestyle; spending concentrated around shifts off.
general-maintenance-and-repair-workers: Apartment complexes, schools, or commercial properties handling varied repairs. Physically active generalist role; responds to diverse maintenance requests. Moderate income; often includes housing benefit at apartment properties; invests in tools.
general-office-clerks: Offices across industries performing varied administrative support tasks. Desk-based with general computer and phone work. Below-average income; often entry-level; limited savings and spending capacity.
genetic-counselors: Hospitals or specialty genetics clinics advising patients on hereditary conditions. Clinical and counseling environment with emotional patient conversations. High income; mission-driven; invests in ABGC board prep and genetics conference attendance.
geographers: Government agencies or universities studying spatial relationships and mapping. Office-based with GIS software and occasional fieldwork. Moderate income; invests in GIS certifications and geographic research tools.
geological-and-petroleum-technicians: Mining or oil companies assisting geoscientists with data collection and lab analysis. Field and lab work; often remote or offshore sites. Good income especially in oil sector; high remote-site spending allowances.
geoscientists: Energy companies, government agencies, or universities studying Earth's physical structure. Field surveys combined with office data analysis; remote sites common. High income in energy sector; invests in field equipment and geoscience conferences.
glaziers: Construction sites or glass shops installing windows, skylights, and storefronts. Physically demanding work with heavy glass panels; height work common. Moderate income; invests in specialty glazing tools and safety equipment.
graphic-designers: Design agencies, marketing departments, or freelance producing visual communications. Desk-based creative work with Adobe Creative Suite. Moderate income; invests in design software subscriptions and online portfolio tools.
grounds-maintenance-workers: Residential properties, parks, or commercial campuses maintaining lawns and landscaping. Outdoor physical work; seasonal demand in northern climates. Below-average income; often Latino workforce; spends on transportation to job sites.
hand-laborers-and-material-movers: Warehouses, construction sites, or loading docks manually moving materials. Heavy physical work; high injury risk; often temp or day labor. Low income; limited savings; essential workers with high physical toll.
hazardous-materials-removal-workers: Construction or industrial sites removing asbestos, lead, or other hazardous materials. Strict safety gear requirement; regulated exposure environment. Moderate income with hazard pay; invests in PPE and OSHA certification.
health-and-safety-engineers: Industrial, construction, or manufacturing companies preventing workplace accidents. Regular plant floor and site walkthroughs combined with office policy work. High income; purchases safety equipment and OSHA certification courses.
health-educators: Public health departments, hospitals, or schools developing and delivering wellness programs. Community outreach combined with program planning and evaluation. Moderate income; invests in CHES certification and health promotion materials.
health-information-technologists-and-med: Hospitals or health systems managing electronic health records and patient data. Desk-based behind-the-scenes clinical support role. Moderate income; invests in RHIA/RHIT certifications and HIM professional organizations.
heating-air-conditioning-and-refrigerati: Residential and commercial buildings installing and servicing HVAC systems. Seasonal demand spikes in summer and winter; mix of new install and service calls. Good income with EPA certification premium; invests in refrigerant tools and diagnostics.
heavy-and-tractor-trailer-truck-drivers: Long-haul trucking companies transporting freight across states. Extended time away from home; sedentary driving with physical loading. Good income; significant spending on truck stop meals, fuel, and CB/GPS equipment.
heavy-vehicle-and-mobile-equipment-servi: Construction, agriculture, or mining operations maintaining heavy equipment. Outdoor field service and shop-based repair; physically demanding. Good income; invests in hydraulic diagnostic tools and manufacturer training.
high-school-teachers: Public or private high schools teaching academic subjects to teenagers. Classroom management, lesson planning, and grading dominate daily schedule. Moderate income; strong benefits and pension; invests in classroom supplies and professional development.
historians: Universities, government, or museums researching and interpreting the past. Archive research and academic writing environment; frequent travel to libraries and archives. Moderate income; often adjunct positions; invests in historical publications and travel.
home-health-aides-and-personal-care-aide: Client homes providing personal care and daily living assistance to elderly or disabled individuals. Physically demanding; travel between client homes; irregular hours. Low income with minimal benefits; limited savings; high transportation costs.
human-resources-managers: Corporate HR leadership setting people strategy and managing HR teams. Office-based senior role with significant cross-functional collaboration. High income; invests in leadership coaching, HR technology platforms, and SHRM membership.
human-resources-specialists: Corporate HR departments recruiting, onboarding, and supporting employees. Desk-based with frequent meetings and HRIS software usage. Stable mid-level income; spends on LinkedIn Premium and professional HR certifications.
hydrologists: Government agencies or consulting firms studying water systems and quality. Field monitoring combined with office-based modeling and reporting. Good income; invests in hydrological modeling software and field equipment.
industrial-designers: Product companies or design consultancies creating concepts for consumer and industrial products. Creative studio work combining sketching, CAD, and prototyping. Good income; invests in 3D modeling software and materials testing tools.
industrial-engineering-technicians: Manufacturing or logistics companies assisting industrial engineers with process optimization. Production floor and office split; time-motion and efficiency analysis. Moderate income; values process improvement; invests in Lean/Six Sigma training.
industrial-engineers: Manufacturing, healthcare, or logistics firms designing efficient systems and processes. Office analysis with frequent floor walks and process observation. High income; invests in IE software and Six Sigma/Lean certifications.
industrial-machinery-mechanics-and-maint: Manufacturing plants maintaining and repairing production machinery. Shift-based factory floor work; critical role in minimizing downtime. Good income; union wages common; invests in machine tool sets and PLC training.
industrial-production-managers: Manufacturing plants overseeing production lines, workers, and output quality. Factory floor and office split; shift work sometimes required. High income; spends on lean manufacturing training and safety equipment.
information-clerks: Hotels, hospitals, or government offices providing information and directing visitors. Front-desk customer-facing service role. Below-average income; values stable hours; spends on professional appearance.
information-security-analysts: Corporate IT, government, or cybersecurity firms protecting data and systems. Office and monitoring environment; high alert during security incidents. High income; significant investment in security certifications (CISSP, CEH) and training.
instructional-coordinators: School districts or educational publishers developing and implementing curriculum. Office-based with school visits and teacher training sessions. Moderate-to-good income; invests in instructional design tools and education research.
insulation-workers: Construction sites installing thermal and acoustic insulation in buildings. Physically demanding work in confined or dusty spaces. Moderate income; often union wages; spends on specialized PPE.
insurance-sales-agents: Insurance companies or independent agencies selling policies to individuals and businesses. Client prospecting, relationship management, and policy quoting. Commission-heavy income; high earning potential but variable; spends on lead generation tools.
insurance-underwriters: Insurance companies assessing risk and setting policy terms. Desk-based analysis role with detailed data review and actuarial tables. Stable income with potential bonuses; moderate spending habits; values job security.
interior-designers: Design firms or independent practice creating interior spaces for clients. Client-facing project work combining creative vision with practical execution. Moderate-to-good income; significant spending on materials samples and industry events.
interpreters-and-translators: Courts, hospitals, international organizations, or freelance providing language services. High cognitive demand; often freelance with variable schedule. Moderate income; invests in terminology databases and professional association membership.
structural-iron-and-steel-workers: Construction and industrial sites erecting steel frameworks for buildings and bridges. Height work and heavy lifting; physically demanding and weather-exposed. Good income with union wages; invests in safety equipment; values skilled trade brotherhood.
janitors-and-building-cleaners: Office buildings, schools, or facilities cleaning and maintaining indoor spaces. Physical shift-based cleaning work; night shifts common. Below-average income; limited benefits; essential workers with minimal discretionary spending.
jewelers-and-precious-stone-and-metal-wo: Jewelry stores or craft workshops designing and repairing jewelry. Fine manual work under magnification; often self-employed or small shop. Moderate income; invests in precision tools and gemological training.
judges-and-hearing-officers: Federal, state, or local courts presiding over legal cases and administrative hearings. High-responsibility professional work; strong public service ethic. High income; strong government benefits; conservative spending habits.
kindergarten-and-elementary-school-teach: Elementary schools nurturing young children's academic and social development. Classroom-based with significant personal spending on supplies. Moderate income; strong pension benefits; high emotional investment in student outcomes.
labor-relations-specialists: Corporate or government setting managing union negotiations and employee relations. Office role with meeting-heavy schedule; occasional travel for negotiations. Well-compensated; regular purchasers of legal and HR continuing education.
landscape-architects: Design firms or government agencies planning outdoor spaces and urban landscapes. Creative studio work combined with site visits. Mid-to-high income; spends on design software and plant/material samples.
lawyers: Law firms, corporations, or government agencies providing legal advice and representation. Long hours; billable hour pressure in private practice; intellectually demanding. High income; significant student debt; high spending on professional development and business development.
librarians: Public libraries, schools, or academic institutions managing collections and research services. Community-facing desk and program work; values information literacy. Moderate income; invests in ALA membership and library science continuing education.
library-technicians-and-assistants: Public libraries or school libraries supporting librarians with circulation and patron services. Counter-based community-serving role; low-key work environment. Low-to-moderate income; values community service; limited discretionary spending.
licensed-practical-and-licensed-vocation: Nursing homes, hospitals, or clinics providing basic patient care under RN supervision. Shift work with physical demands; emotionally challenging environment. Moderate income; often pursuing RN bridge programs; invests in NCLEX prep materials.
loan-officers: Banks or mortgage companies evaluating loan applications and advising borrowers. Mix of office and client-facing work; income tied to commission on closed loans. Higher earning potential in hot real estate markets; professional wardrobe expected.
lodging-managers: Hotels or resorts managing operations, staff, and guest experience. Long irregular hours including weekends; hospitality-focused environment. Moderate income; often includes housing benefits; spends on customer service training.
logging-workers: Timber companies felling and processing trees in commercial logging operations. Physically demanding dangerous work; remote site with seasonal patterns. Good income for skill level; high injury risk; spends on chainsaw equipment and safety gear.
logisticians: Supply chain and logistics roles in manufacturing, retail, or defense. Office-based with heavy ERP software usage and vendor communication. Stable mid-to-high income; stress spikes during supply chain disruptions.
machinists-and-tool-and-die-makers: Machine shops or manufacturing plants creating precision metal parts. High-skill machining with CNC equipment and traditional tools. Good income; significant investment in personal toolbox and CNC programming skills.
management-analysts: Consulting firms or internal strategy teams advising organizations on efficiency. Heavy travel, PowerPoint presentations, and client meetings. High income potential; invests in business attire, executive MBA programs, and networking.
manicurists-and-pedicurists: Nail salons providing manicure, pedicure, and nail art services. Standing-intensive client-facing work; chemical exposure to nail products. Below-average income with tips; often Vietnamese-American small business community. Invests in nail art tools and salon products.
marine-engineers-and-naval-architects: Shipyards, defense contractors, or shipping companies designing vessels and marine systems. Office design work combined with shipyard and sea trial visits. High income; niche field; spends on maritime engineering conferences.
market-research-analysts: Corporate marketing departments or research agencies collecting and interpreting data. Desk-based with heavy data analysis and survey tool usage. Mid-level income; values technical skills training and marketing certifications.
marriage-and-family-therapists: Private practice or community mental health centers treating relationship and family issues. Intensive client-facing therapy sessions; emotionally demanding work. Moderate income; significant investment in licensure (LMFT) and clinical supervision.
brickmasons-blockmasons-and-stonemasons: Construction sites laying bricks, blocks, and stone for structures. Physically demanding outdoor work; seasonal in northern climates. Good income; often self-employed; invests in masonry tools and truck.
massage-therapists: Spas, chiropractic clinics, or independent practice performing therapeutic massages. Physically demanding; part-time and commission-based income common. Moderate income; values self-employment flexibility; spends on continuing education and table equipment.
material-moving-machine-operators: Warehouses, ports, or construction sites operating forklifts and cranes. Shift-based machine operation; safety-critical environment. Moderate income; invests in forklift certifications and PPE.
material-recording-clerks: Warehouses, hospitals, or distribution centers tracking inventory and shipments. Physical and desk-based inventory management work. Below-average income; shift work; limited discretionary spending.
materials-engineers: Manufacturing, aerospace, or research institutions developing metals, plastics, and composites. Lab-heavy work with materials testing equipment. High income; invests in materials science journals and technical conferences.
mathematicians-and-statisticians: Government agencies, research institutions, or financial firms applying mathematical methods. Abstract analytical work; publication and research grants in academia. High income in industry; moderate in academia; invests in statistical software (SAS, R).
mechanical-engineering-technicians: Manufacturing or engineering firms building and testing mechanical designs. Hands-on lab and production floor work. Moderate income; spends on machining tools and technical certifications.
mechanical-engineers: Manufacturing, energy, or defense companies designing machines and mechanical systems. Office and lab environment; broad career paths across industries. High income; invests in CAD software (SolidWorks) and ASME memberships.
medical-and-health-services-managers: Hospitals, clinics, or healthcare systems managing departments or entire facilities. Office-based leadership with high regulatory and compliance burden. High income; significant investment in healthcare administration certifications (MHA, MBA).
medical-assistants: Outpatient clinics performing administrative and clinical support tasks. Busy patient-flow environment with multi-tasking demands. Below-average income; often a stepping stone to nursing or other healthcare careers.
medical-dosimetrists: Cancer treatment centers calculating radiation doses for patient treatment plans. Highly technical desk and planning system work in oncology department. High income; niche specialty; invests in dosimetry certifications and physics coursework.
medical-equipment-repairers: Hospitals or medical equipment companies maintaining and repairing clinical devices. Hands-on technical work in clinical settings; on-call for urgent repairs. Good income; invests in biomedical equipment technician (BMET) certifications.
medical-records-and-health-information-t: Hospital or clinic HIM departments coding diagnoses and managing patient records. Desk-based; often remote work possible; heavy EHR software usage. Moderate income; invests in ICD coding certifications (CPC, CCS).
medical-scientists: Research universities, pharma companies, or government labs conducting biomedical research. Lab-intensive environment; grant-funded projects drive timelines. High income in industry; lower in academia; invests in journal subscriptions and conferences.
medical-transcriptionists: Remote or healthcare office setting transcribing physician dictations into medical records. Home-based desk work; independent contractor arrangements common. Below-average income; declining field; values flexibility; limited spending capacity.
meeting-convention-and-event-planners: Hotels, corporations, or independent event firms coordinating large-scale events. High-pressure, deadline-driven role with travel during event execution. Moderate income with significant vendor and venue spending; values flexible scheduling.
metal-and-plastic-machine-workers: Manufacturing plants operating metal cutting, forming, or plastic molding machines. Repetitive machine operation; shift-based; safety-critical environment. Moderate income; union wages in some sectors; spends carefully on essentials.
microbiologists: Pharma, food safety, or research institutions studying microorganisms. Lab-intensive work with culture media and microscopy equipment. High income in industry; moderate in academia; invests in ASM membership and lab tools.
middle-school-teachers: Middle schools teaching academic subjects to pre-teenagers. Energetic classroom environment; requires strong classroom management skills. Moderate income with good benefits; invests in subject-area resources and teaching strategies.
military-careers: Active duty military in Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, or Coast Guard. Structured hierarchical environment with mandatory deployments and relocations. Moderate base pay with housing, food, and healthcare benefits provided; career advancement tied to performance and time-in-service; values discipline and service.
mining-and-geological-engineers: Mining companies or consulting firms designing safe and efficient extraction operations. Remote mine sites combined with office planning work; physically demanding. High income; remote location spending patterns; invests in safety and mining engineering courses.
models: Modeling agencies, fashion shows, or commercial photography clients representing products. Project-based freelance income; travel and appearance demands. Variable income; high spending on wardrobe, fitness, and agency fees.
music-directors-and-composers: Orchestras, studios, or film companies leading musical ensembles or creating original scores. Creative and conducting work; project-based income supplemented by teaching. Moderate income; invests in musical instruments, software (DAWs), and scores.
musicians-and-singers: Concert venues, recording studios, or events performing music. Irregular project-based income; touring involves extended time away from home. Variable income; significant investment in instruments, recording equipment, and music production.
natural-sciences-managers: Research institutions, pharma companies, or government labs leading scientific teams. Lab and office split; grant writing and budget management are core duties. High income; invests in scientific journals, conferences, and research tools.
network-and-computer-systems-administrat: IT departments managing servers, networks, and users across organizations. Office and data center work with on-call responsibilities. Good income; invests in Microsoft and Cisco certifications; early adopter of home tech.
reporters-correspondents-and-broadcast-n: News outlets covering events and reporting stories across print, digital, and broadcast. Irregular hours; deadline pressure; field reporting with travel. Moderate income; declining job market; invests in journalism tools and network relationships.
nuclear-engineers: Nuclear power plants, government labs, or defense contractors working with nuclear technology. Highly regulated, safety-critical environment; mostly office-based design and analysis. High income; strong job security; values stability over lifestyle spending.
nuclear-medicine-technologists: Hospitals or imaging centers administering radioactive drugs and imaging studies. Clinical environment with radiation safety protocols; variable shift schedules. High income; invests in NMTCB certification and continuing education.
nuclear-technicians: Nuclear power plants or research facilities operating radiation monitoring equipment. Safety-critical shift work with strict radiation exposure protocols. High income; strong union benefits; values job security; limited risk-taking.
nurse-anesthetists-nurse-midwives-and-nu: Hospitals, surgery centers, or independent practice providing advanced clinical care. High-stakes patient-facing work with significant autonomy. Very high income; heavy investment in advanced degree and board certification; high discretionary spending.
nursing-assistants: Nursing homes, hospitals, or rehabilitation centers providing basic patient care. Physically demanding; shift work including nights and weekends. Low income; limited benefits; significant physical and emotional toll; minimal savings.
occupational-health-and-safety-specialis: Manufacturing, construction, or corporate settings identifying and reducing workplace hazards. Regular facility inspections and safety training delivery. Moderate income; invests in CSP/CHST certification and safety equipment.
occupational-therapists: Hospitals, schools, or private practice helping patients regain daily living skills. Active patient-facing work with therapeutic activities and assistive technology. High income; invests in NBCOT certification and continuing education credits.
occupational-therapy-assistants-and-aide: Rehabilitation facilities or schools assisting occupational therapists with patient care. Hands-on patient work; physically active environment. Moderate income; values helping patients; often pursuing OT degrees for advancement.
oil-and-gas-workers: Oil rigs, refineries, or pipeline construction sites; physically demanding energy sector work. Remote sites with rotation schedules (weeks on, weeks off). Good income with field pay; concentrated spending on time off after long rotations.
operations-research-analysts: Government, military, or corporate operations using advanced analytics to solve logistics problems. Office-based modeling work with stakeholder communication. High income; invests in operations research software and INFORMS membership.
opticians-dispensing: Optical retail stores or eye clinics fitting and adjusting eyeglasses and contact lenses. Customer-facing retail environment; regular standing and fitting work. Below-average income; may receive discounts on eyewear; spends on opticianry certification.
optometrists: Own practice or optical chains providing comprehensive eye exams and vision correction. Clinical appointment-based schedule with own equipment investment. High income; significant student debt and practice overhead; spends on ophthalmic equipment.
orthotists-and-prosthetists: Clinical or private practice designing and fitting orthotic and prosthetic devices. Technical and patient-facing work; each device is custom-made. High income; mission-driven; invests in ABC certification and materials.
painters-construction-and-maintenance: Residential and commercial properties applying paint and coatings. Physical work; exposure to fumes; independent contractors common. Moderate income; often self-employed; invests in sprayers, ladders, and a work vehicle.
painting-and-coating-workers: Manufacturing or industrial facilities applying paint and protective coatings to products. Repetitive work with chemical exposure; PPE required. Moderate income; shift work; invests in PPE and spray equipment maintenance.
paralegals-and-legal-assistants: Law firms or corporate legal departments supporting attorneys with research and documentation. Desk-based with deadline pressure around court filings and closings. Moderate income; invests in CLA/CP certification and legal research tools.
personal-financial-advisors: Wealth management firms, banks, or independent practice advising clients on investments. Client-facing with significant relationship-building and portfolio management. Income tied to assets under management; high earners invest in their own market presence.
pest-control-workers: Pest control companies treating residential and commercial properties for infestations. Field-based chemical treatment work in varied environments. Moderate income; vehicle-based independent route work; invests in licensing and PPE.
petroleum-engineers: Oil and gas companies designing and optimizing extraction operations. Mix of remote field sites and corporate office work. Very high income with commodity price volatility; high lifestyle spending when times are good.
pharmacists: Retail pharmacies, hospitals, or clinical settings dispensing medications and counseling patients. High accuracy, high-volume dispensing environment. Very high income; heavy student debt burden; spends on pharmacology journals and CE.
pharmacy-technicians: Retail pharmacies or hospital settings assisting pharmacists with dispensing. High-volume, detail-oriented counter work; shift-based schedule. Below-average income; often a career entry point; invests in PTCB certification.
phlebotomists: Hospitals, labs, or blood drives collecting blood specimens from patients. Patient-facing clinical role; standing-intensive with needlework. Below-average income; entry-level healthcare role; spends on clinical supplies.
photographers: Portrait studios, news agencies, or freelance creating visual imagery for clients. Project-based freelance income; equipment-intensive business model. Variable income; high camera and lens investment; spends on marketing and portfolio development.
physical-therapist-assistants-and-aides: Rehabilitation centers or hospitals helping physical therapists with patient treatment. Active physical work; patient encouragement and mobility assistance. Moderate income; often pursuing PTA licensure; values patient outcomes.
physical-therapists: Hospitals, private clinics, or sports medicine centers restoring patient mobility and function. Active patient-facing work; physically demanding with manual therapy techniques. High income; significant student debt; invests in continuing education and specialty certifications.
physician-assistants: Hospitals, clinics, or specialty practices providing medical diagnosis and treatment. High autonomy clinical work; demanding but intellectually engaging. Very high income; invests in NCCPA certification maintenance and medical education.
physicians-and-surgeons: Hospitals, private practice, or academic medical centers providing comprehensive medical care. Long training pathway; high-stress, high-responsibility work environment. Very high income; significant student debt; high spending on professional dues, insurance, and lifestyle.
physicists-and-astronomers: Research universities, national labs, or government agencies studying fundamental physics. Lab or observatory-based research with heavy computational analysis. High income in industry; moderate in academia; invests in scientific computing and conferences.
plumbers-pipefitters-and-steamfitters: Residential, commercial, and industrial settings installing and repairing piping systems. Mix of new construction and emergency service calls. High income with union wages; on-call premium pay; invests in pipe tools and vans.
podiatrists: Private practice or hospital podiatry departments treating foot and ankle conditions. Clinical appointment-based schedule; mix of conservative and surgical care. High income; manages private practice overhead; invests in surgical training.
police-and-detectives: Local, state, or federal law enforcement agencies maintaining public safety. Shift-based patrol and investigation work; physically and emotionally demanding. Good income with benefits; government pension; invests in physical fitness and tactical training.
political-scientists: Universities, think tanks, or government agencies studying political systems and behavior. Research writing and data analysis environment; publication-driven academic culture. High income in policy institutes; moderate in academia; invests in political data services.
postal-service-workers: USPS processing and delivery facilities sorting and delivering mail. Physical outdoor and facility-based work; shift-based. Moderate income with strong federal benefits; union protection; values job security.
postsecondary-education-administrators: Colleges or universities managing registrar, student services, or academic departments. Office-based with committee meetings and policy management. Moderate-to-good income; strong benefits; invests in higher education leadership development.
postsecondary-teachers: Colleges or universities teaching courses and conducting research. Mix of teaching, research, and service in academic departments. Moderate to high income depending on tenure status; heavy investment in research and publishing.
power-plant-operators-distributors-and-d: Power generating facilities monitoring and controlling electricity generation equipment. Shift-based high-attention work; critical infrastructure responsibility. High income; union wages; strong job security; values stability and retirement benefits.
preschool-and-childcare-center-directors: Early childhood centers managing curriculum, licensing, staff, and parent relations. Hands-on facility management with heavy administrative load. Moderate income; invests in early childhood education resources and licensing compliance.
preschool-teachers: Preschools or daycare centers providing early childhood education. Energetic physical work with young children; circle time and play-based learning. Low income; high passion; spends on classroom materials and child development books.
private-detectives-and-investigators: Private investigation agencies or independent practice conducting surveillance and research. Field-based often solo work; irregular hours following subjects. Moderate income; often former law enforcement; spends on surveillance equipment.
probation-officers-and-correctional-trea: Courts or correctional agencies supervising offenders and developing rehabilitation plans. Field-based caseload management with safety risks and emotional demands. Moderate income; strong government benefits; invests in social work CEUs.
producers-and-directors: Film studios, TV networks, or theater companies overseeing production of creative content. Project-driven leadership work with team management and budget responsibility. Moderate-to-high income; invests in industry networking and production tools.
project-management-specialists: Corporate or government offices managing timelines, budgets, and cross-team deliverables. Desk-based with heavy meeting and project software (JIRA, MS Project) usage. Stable mid-to-high income; PMP certification is a common career investment.
appraisers-and-assessors-of-real-estate: Real estate or government offices assessing property values for transactions or taxation. Mix of fieldwork visiting properties and office-based report writing. Income tied to real estate activity; independent business expenses are common.
property-real-estate-and-community-assoc: Property management firms or HOAs overseeing residential or commercial properties. Field-based visiting properties and meeting owners or tenants. Moderate income; spending tied to property maintenance budgets and realtor networks.
psychiatric-technicians-and-aides: Mental health facilities or hospitals supporting psychiatric patients with daily activities. Emotionally demanding shift work; potential for workplace incidents. Below-average income; values mission over pay; limited discretionary spending.
psychologists: Private practice, hospitals, schools, or research institutions studying behavior and mental health. Clinical or research environment; emotionally demanding patient or subject interactions. High income in clinical practice; lower in research; heavy investment in licensure and CE.
public-relations-managers: Corporate communications, agencies, or nonprofits managing reputation and media strategy. High-visibility role with frequent media and stakeholder interactions. High income; spends on media monitoring tools, industry events, and executive presence coaching.
public-relations-specialists: Corporate communications, agencies, or nonprofits managing organizational reputation. Writing-heavy role with media relationship management. Moderate-to-good income; invests in PRSA membership and media monitoring tools.
police-fire-and-ambulance-dispatchers: Emergency communications centers coordinating emergency response units. High-stress shift work monitoring multiple radio channels. Moderate income with government benefits; invests in EMD certification and stress management.
purchasing-managers-buyers-and-purchasin: Retail, manufacturing, or corporate procurement managing supplier relationships and contracts. Office-based with travel to trade shows and supplier sites. Mid-to-high income; strong negotiation culture; regular purchases of industry publications.
quality-control-inspectors: Manufacturing or food processing facilities inspecting products for defects. Repetitive inspection work with testing equipment. Moderate income; invests in quality certifications (ASQ CQT) and measurement tools.
radiation-therapists: Cancer treatment centers delivering prescribed radiation treatments to patients. Clinical environment with precise equipment operation; emotionally supportive patient interactions. High income; invests in ARRT certification and oncology continuing education.
radiologic-technologists: Hospitals or imaging centers performing X-rays and MRI scans. Shift-based clinical work with heavy equipment operation. Good income; invests in ARRT certification and imaging technology updates.
railroad-occupations: Freight or passenger rail companies operating trains and maintaining rail infrastructure. Shift-based away-from-home assignments; irregular hours. Good income with union wages; strong retirement benefits; invests in transportation union membership.
real-estate-brokers-and-sales-agents: Real estate agencies buying and selling properties for clients. Commission-based income tied to transaction volume; highly variable with market. Variable income; high in hot markets; significant spending on marketing, vehicles, and licensing.
receptionists: Corporate offices, medical practices, or hotels greeting visitors and managing phone lines. Front-desk customer-facing role; often part-time. Below-average income; values stable environment; spends on professional appearance.
recreation-workers: Parks, community centers, or resorts organizing recreational activities for groups. Active outdoor or facility-based programming work. Below-average income; values community engagement; spends on recreational equipment.
recreational-therapists: Hospitals, rehab centers, or long-term care facilities using recreation to improve patient wellbeing. Active, creative work with therapeutic program development. Moderate income; mission-driven; invests in CTRS certification and program supplies.
registered-nurses: Hospitals, clinics, or community health settings providing direct patient care. Shift-based; physically and emotionally demanding; high patient interaction. Good income; invests in specialty certifications (CCRN, CEN) and BSN-to-MSN education.
rehabilitation-counselors: Vocational rehabilitation agencies helping people with disabilities find employment. Case management and job placement counseling. Moderate income; mission-driven; invests in CRC certification and assistive technology knowledge.
respiratory-therapists: Hospitals or pulmonary clinics treating patients with breathing disorders. Shift-based ICU and ward work; critical care environment. Good income; invests in RRT certification and respiratory care continuing education.
retail-sales-workers: Retail stores assisting customers and completing sales transactions. Shift-based customer-facing work including weekends and holidays. Low income; often part-time; spends on transportation to work and work attire.
roofers: Residential and commercial buildings installing and repairing roofing systems. Outdoor height work; physically demanding; seasonal in northern climates. Moderate income; often subcontractor; invests in safety equipment and roofing tools.
sales-engineers: Technology or industrial companies selling complex products requiring technical expertise. Client-facing technical presentations and relationship management with travel. High income; significant bonus tied to quota; invests in technical certifications and client entertainment.
sales-managers: Corporate sales departments setting targets and leading sales teams. High-energy performance-driven environment; frequent travel for client meetings. High income with significant bonus potential; spends on CRM tools, incentive travel, and client entertainment.
school-and-career-counselors: K-12 schools or college career centers supporting student academic and career development. Busy caseloads with one-on-one counseling and group programming. Moderate income; school calendar schedule; invests in career counseling tools.
secretaries-and-administrative-assistant: Corporate offices or professional practices supporting executives and teams. Desk-based multitasking with scheduling, correspondence, and coordination. Moderate income; invests in Microsoft Office certification and project management skills.
securities-commodities-and-financial-ser: Brokerage firms, banks, or financial advisories selling investment products. Client relationship management and market monitoring. High income with commission; invests in Series 7/66 licenses; high lifestyle spending.
security-guards: Commercial buildings, events, or government facilities monitoring and protecting premises. Shift-based patrol and monitoring work; often standing or mobile. Low income; often part-time; limited spending capacity; values stable employment.
semiconductor-processing-technicians: Semiconductor fabrication plants operating equipment to manufacture chips. Cleanroom environment; strict protocols; shift-based. Moderate income; tech industry benefits; invests in technical skills and certifications.
set-and-exhibit-designers: Entertainment studios, theater companies, or museums designing physical environments. Creative project work with physical construction and technical staging. Moderate income; project-based employment; invests in design software and model-making materials.
sheet-metal-workers: HVAC, construction, or manufacturing sites fabricating and installing sheet metal systems. Skilled trade combining shop fabrication and field installation. Good income with union wages; invests in specialty tools and metal working equipment.
skincare-specialists: Spas, salons, or medical aesthetics clinics providing facials and skin treatments. Hands-on client service; physically active and relationship-driven. Moderate income; invests in esthetics licensure continuing education and professional skincare products.
small-engine-mechanics: Equipment repair shops or dealers servicing lawnmowers, motorcycles, and small engines. Shop-based technical work; seasonal demand peaks in spring and summer. Moderate income; often small business owners; invests in diagnostic tools.
social-and-community-service-managers: Nonprofits or government agencies running social programs and community services. Mix of community fieldwork and office management; grant reporting is central. Moderate income; mission-driven; invests in nonprofit management certifications.
social-and-human-service-assistants: Social service agencies assisting case managers with client support and resources. Community fieldwork; emotionally taxing client interactions. Below-average income; strong sense of purpose; limited savings capacity.
social-workers: Child welfare agencies, hospitals, or nonprofits supporting vulnerable populations. High caseload; emotionally demanding fieldwork and crisis intervention. Moderate income relative to education; high student debt; invests in LCSW licensure.
sociologists: Universities or research organizations studying social behavior and institutions. Academic research and writing environment; fieldwork for qualitative studies. Moderate income; often grant-dependent; invests in research software (SPSS, NVivo).
